ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a beef brisket grain bowl. FOODON_00002473 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for a composed beef brisket bowl with grains and vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Beef dish) is used as the closest parent. No specific concept exists for a beef brisket energy bowl.

allergens: gluten (possible from sauce or grain base)

serving.natural_language_portion: One ala carte energy bowl serving of approximately 380 g, comprising a grain base, braised beef brisket, mixed salad vegetables, and a sauce or dressing, as served at Subway or similar fast-casual outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef brisket, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13326); supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods grain bowl component estimates and HPB Singapore nutrient reference for mixed grain bowls
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composed beef brisket energy bowl as sold at Subway Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by component assembly: braised beef brisket (USDA SR Legacy NDB 13326, ~40% of bowl weight), multigrain/brown rice base (~35%), mixed salad vegetables (~20%), and sauce/dressing (~5%). Per-100g values are weighted composites. This is a medium-confidence estimate. HPB Singapore Foodo was consulted but does not carry this specific commercial item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; brown rice GI ~50–55 used as primary GI anchor for grain base component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composed beef brisket energy bowl. GI is estimated at 52 (low) based on the dominant grain base component (brown rice / multigrain, GI ~50–55 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN international GI tables). Braised beef brisket and salad vegetables contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 51.3) / 100 ≈ 26.7, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving ≈ 51.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1178 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this bowl as a single serving may contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg; requesting reduced sauce or dressing can meaningfully lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~26.7 per serving) driven by grain base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note the moderate glycemic load of approximately 26.7 per serving; choosing a smaller grain portion or substituting with a lower-GI base such as barley can help attenuate postprandial glucose response, while the high protein content (~42.6 g per serving) supports satiety and glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g; ~8 g per serving) and sodium from braised brisket and sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat from beef brisket and the elevated sodium from braising sauces represent the primary concerns; limiting portion size, requesting sauce on the side, and increasing the vegetable proportion can reduce cardiovascular risk associated with this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Energy Bowl with Beef Brisket is a fast-casual grain bowl dish sold ala carte at Subway Singapore and similar Mix & Match outlets. It consists of a base of multigrain or brown rice topped with slow-braised beef brisket, mixed salad greens, and assorted vegetables, finished with a sauce or dressing. The beef brisket is typically braised until tender, yielding a rich, savoury flavour with a soft, pull-apart texture. The grain base provides complex carbohydrates and dietary fibre, while the brisket contributes substantial protein and iron. Per 100 g, the bowl provides approximately 148 kcal, 11.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 13.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notable at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, largely from braising sauces, making it a moderate-sodium meal. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category) based on the brown rice or multigrain grain base. Clinically, the bowl is a moderate-concern food for hypertension due to sodium, for Type 2 Diabetes due to its moderate glycemic load of approximately 26.7 per serving, and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from the brisket. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Halal status depends on the specific restaurant and sourcing. This dish is popular in Singapore's fast-casual Western dining segment and is typically priced around SGD 11.90 ala carte.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific commercial product as sold at Subway Singapore or Funan Mix & Match. Nutrient values are estimated by component assembly using USDA SR Legacy data for braised beef brisket and USDA Foundation Foods data for grain and vegetable components. GI is estimated from the closest analogous grain base (brown rice/multigrain) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the assembly methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this type of composed commercial bowl in the source databases used. 2. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for braised beef brisket and was not estimable with confidence from the vegetable components. 3. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component assembly. 4. Sodium is elevated (~310 mg/100g) primarily due to braising sauces and seasoning; actual restaurant values may vary. 5. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed from available public information; individual restaurants may hold halal certification. 6. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical fast-casual grain bowl portions; actual weight may vary by outlet. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate anchored to brown rice/multigrain base per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composed dish. 8. This record covers the ala carte version only; combo or set meal versions are not included.
